reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The equity-NGO voice defends access to quality education for disadvantaged and Roma pupils, and opposes structural segregation. S1 addresses neither root cause — early selection and the concentration of low-SES pupils in general schools — but offers mechanical quota gains IF the SES indicator is built and IF outreach reaches pupils currently unaware of selective tracks. This is conditional support: the quota mechanism is sound in principle, but the conditions are known to be fragile.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils, particularly those in under-applying settlements, and their families' access to selective academic tracks without re-segregation.
reactions.public_good_frame: Equal opportunity to quality education and reduction of SES-driven educational segregation within the formal structure.
reactions.argument: We support the quota mechanism because it mechanically reserves seats for low-SES applicants — a direct tool — and because documented outreach can activate demand in settlements where information barriers are real (we have seen this work at small scale). However, this scenario depends on three fragile conditions: (1) a non-gameable SES indicator that does not yet exist and that Hungary has no administrative register to build from; (2) outreach that overcomes not just information gaps but deep structural barriers (transport, family confidence, prior exposure) that are often understated; (3) the assumption that reformed assessment design can resist coaching, which the scenario itself admits is undemonstrated. The core problem — that selection happens at age 10, before many low-SES and Roma families even know selective tracks exist — is not solved; S1 only reweights applicants who already apply. The bulk of low-SES pupils who never apply remain untouched. Worst case: informal selection (interviews, reputation) reproduces SES sorting within the reformed channel, and middle-class mobilisation against quotas undermines the reform. Best case: 15-20% of reserved seats reach high-ability low-SES pupils who would otherwise be excluded. We have seen both outcomes.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would strengthen support if: (a) a pilot SES indicator is tested and proven non-gameable in at least three settlements; (b) qualitative research shows information, not structural barriers, drives underapplication in target settlements; (c) the new assessment is independently validated as coaching-resistant in a rigorous psychometric pilot; (d) the quota mechanism includes explicit safeguards against informal selection (no interviews as tie-breakers, transparent criteria). We would oppose if: (e) the SES indicator is built but found to be easily gameable; (f) outreach campaigns reach <50% of target families; (g) informal selection emerges in reformed channels and no audit mechanism is in place to detect it.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The equity-NGO voice opposes early selection and residential/school segregation. S2 shrinks selective seats without removing selection at age 10-12, and without any admission-rule reform to make the residual seats less SES-skewed. The mechanical effect is immediate: fewer seats + same applicant pool = even more SES-concentrated selection. Non-state maintainers will absorb displaced demand, re-locating sorting rather than removing it. The scenario assumes this will not happen, but the scenario itself documents that it will. Most critically: S2 does not serve disadvantaged or Roma pupils — it tightens competition for the same skewed pool of selective seats.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils, and their access to selective tracks and high-achieving peers.
reactions.public_good_frame: Early sorting is a driver of lifelong educational inequality; shrinking seats without structural reform simply makes the sorting mechanism more brutal, not less.
reactions.argument: We oppose S2 because it worsens equity without reforming the sorting mechanism itself. The scenario's own risk analysis states: 'As caps tighten, residual selective places become even more SES-skewed absent an S1-type admission-rule change.' Exactly so. With 8-12% of places reserved for selective entry and no change to admission rules, tightening the cap means high-SES families compete even harder for scarcer seats, and low-SES families face even lower odds. The peer-composition benefit claimed (retaining high-achievers in common track improves outcomes for non-selected pupils) is contested and depends on a weak mechanism — and it only helps pupils already in the common track, not pupils excluded by selection. Non-state maintainers will expand to absorb selective demand: this is not speculation, it is documented in school-network planning and maintainer statistics. S2 does not prevent it; it incentivises it. The result is not fewer sorted pupils but more sorted pupils in church/foundation schools, where less is known about their composition. For disadvantaged and Roma pupils currently shut out of selective tracks, S2 offers nothing; for those applying, it offers a worse lottery. This is why three separate curtailment attempts (1990s-2010s) failed — they were felt as illegitimate precisely because they reduced opportunity without opening it.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would shift to conditional support only if S2 is paired with: (a) an S1-type admission-rule and quota reform that makes residual selective seats less SES-skewed, AND (b) a binding intake cap on non-state maintainers that is enforced with real consequences (not negotiation). Without both, S2 is not a reduction of sorting — it is a hardening of it. We would support if evidence showed that retaining high-achievers in common tracks actually improved outcomes for non-selected low-SES pupils in longitudinal data — but that evidence does not yet exist, and the mechanism is tagged weak/contested.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The equity-NGO voice defends access for disadvantaged and Roma pupils to quality education and high-achieving peers. S3 defers selection to age 14 and removes the early partition that concentrates low-SES pupils in parallel general schools before they are academically or socially stabilised. International evidence (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006; Pekkarinen et al. 2009; Jakubowski et al. 2016) shows delayed selection reduces SES-outcome gradients without harming mean achievement. This aligns with our core interest: deferring the point at which family SES determines school peer composition.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils, and their access to high-achieving peers and quality academic instruction in the critical years ages 10-14.
reactions.public_good_frame: Early tracking at age 10-12 locks in SES advantage before academic or social stabilisation. Deferring selection to 14 and eliminating the parallel-track system removes the mechanism that generates segregation, rather than merely compensating for it.
reactions.argument: S3 is the only scenario that addresses the root cause: early selection at age 10-12 that concentrates disadvantaged pupils in general schools before they have stabilised academically or socially. The evidence base is moderate and consistent across countries (Hungary's specific peer-effect mechanism is contested, but the cross-country delayed-selection association is strong). Most importantly, S3 removes the sorting engine itself, rather than trying to manage its effects downstream. For disadvantaged and Roma pupils, this means: (1) they remain with high-achieving peers through age 14, not separated at 10; (2) teacher expectations are not sorted by track at a formative age; (3) within-school differentiation (if well-resourced) can respond to individual progress rather than early family-SES-driven assignment. The largest risk is real: middle-class and church/foundation exit to private schools can re-concentrate disadvantage in general schools, offsetting the equity gain. But this is a risk that can be managed (binding intake rules with church/foundation maintainers, residential-choice countermeasures) and that is not intrinsic to the reform itself. The Polish reversal (2016-2019) is a political warning, not evidence that delayed selection doesn't work — measured gains were evident before reversal. The teacher-shortage constraint is serious and binding; if unresolved, it will degrade the benefit. But the solution is to fix teacher supply (a separate equity imperative), not to abandon delayed selection.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would remain firm in support, conditional on: (a) binding intake rules with non-state maintainers that cap sorting migration and are enforced with audit; (b) a teacher-retraining programme beginning immediately, with wage incentives to stabilise supply in disadvantaged schools; (c) universal diagnostic support systems in place before comprehensive upper-grade rollout; (d) political commitment across two electoral cycles (contingent on opposition stakeholder buy-in — interviews with Polish reformers on what erodes/maintains support). We would withdraw support only if: (e) non-state maintainer exit is higher than 15% in pilot years and no enforcement mechanism is deployed; (f) teacher supply worsens rather than stabilises; (g) within-school differentiation is implemented without diagnostic support and becomes de facto within-school tracking by SES (i.e., the reform is subverted). We are aware this is a high-cost, high-uncertainty reform; the conditions reflect that.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The equity-NGO voice opposes structural segregation and early selection. S4 retains both — it keeps age-10 and age-12 selection intact and merely adds compensatory funding downstream. This is the non-structural 'separate but equal' approach. No country evidence exists that compensation can offset selection as early as age 10-12 (Portugal did not select at that age). For disadvantaged and Roma pupils, S4 promises funding for the general schools where they are concentrated by selection, but does not remove the selection itself. This entrenches sorting rather than dismantles it.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils, and their access to selective academic tracks and high-achieving peers without structural segregation.
reactions.public_good_frame: Structural segregation is the driver of lifelong inequality. Compensation without desegregation is a mechanism to make segregation tolerable, not to end it.
reactions.argument: We oppose S4 because it accepts the segregation created by age-10 selection and tries to offset it with money. This is the 'separate but equal' logic that has failed in every context where it has been tested. The scenario acknowledges this: 'no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age; the Portuguese case did not alter selection age but also did not select as early as Hungary.' Exactly. Portugal selected later and less severely; even so, TEIP-style compensation did not equalise outcomes between selected and non-selected tracks. Hungary selects at 10-12, earlier and more severely. The promise is that targeted funding and curriculum standards will raise attainment in disadvantaged general schools 'without structural change,' but: (1) the sorting mechanism remains intact — low-SES and Roma pupils are still selected out at 10-12 and concentrated in general schools; (2) teacher shortage (documented as strong constraint) will degrade standard delivery precisely in disadvantaged schools; (3) TEIP-style funding is the first line cut in fiscal consolidation — 'exposure is serious' — so sustainability is low; (4) the peer-composition effect of concentration remains negative for low-SES pupils, regardless of funding. Most critically: S4 legitimises early selection permanently. By offering compensation, it frames segregation as a problem to be managed, not dismantled. This is exactly the framing that makes reform politically harder: middle-class parents accept selection if it is paired with 'good' funding for general schools. Disadvantaged families are left to compete for the same skewed selective seats, with better funding in the schools where they are sorted but no structural change to the sorting itself. We have seen this in other countries: compensation without desegregation entrenches rather than erodes segregation over time.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would not support S4 even with strong conditions, because it does not address the root cause. However, we could accept it as a transitional package only if paired explicitly with S3 (comprehensive school to 14): i.e., S4 buys time and stabilises funding while S3 is legislated and implemented. Without that pairing, S4 is a dead end. A non-negotiable condition would be: (a) a binding commitment that S3 legislation is tabled and debated within the first two years of S4 implementation, with measurable stakeholder engagement; (b) TEIP-style funding is ring-fenced with multi-year budget guarantees (not annual review); (c) within-school differentiation in general schools is audited for SES-based tracking and remedied if detected. Even with all three, S4 alone would be insufficient; it is a holding pattern, not a solution.
